content.full_text:
Opinion Request Meeting Participants 
March 14, 1980 
John Giansello 
Itemization Of Expenditures Made By Agents Or Independent 
Contractors 
A political committee organized principally to support a 
ballot measure has contracted with a campaign management firm 
to run the campaign. 
The campaign management firm contracts 
with a production firm to make television commereials support-
ing the measure. 
The production firm in turn pays the actors 
who participate in the commercials. 
The universal practice 
in the industry is to pay actors who participate in commercials 
in cash. 
The fees of many actors will exceed $100. 
Government Code S84303 requires that no expenditure shall 
be made, other than overhead or normal operating expenses, by 
an agent or independent contractor, including but not limited 
to an advertising agency, on behalf of or for the benefit of 
any candidate or committee unless it is reported by the candi-
date or committee as if the expenditure were made directly 
by the candidate or committee, unless the agent or independent 
contractor files a campaign statment reporting the expenditure. 
The question is·, how far down the chain of payments over 
does this requirement apply? 
Must the committee list the 
payments from the production company to the actors as if they 
were paid by the committee directly? If so, are such payments 
to the actors then "expenditures" within the meaning of the 
Act? 
(They are considered to be made "directly" by the commit-
tee by reason of Govt .• Code §84303. 
All payments made by a 
primary purpose committee even if made for full and adequate 
consideration are "expenditures" per 2 Cal. Admin. Code 
S18225 (b) (4» • 
If the payments to the actors by the production company 
are "expenditures," then is Government Code S84300(a) implicated, 
which prohibits cash expenditures of $100 or more?

sections.question: The question is, how far down the chain of payments does this requirement apply? Must the committee list the payments from the production company to the actors as if they were paid by the committee directly? If so, are such payments to the actors then "expenditures" within the meaning of the Act? If the payments to the actors by the production company are "expenditures," then is Government Code §84300(a) implicated, which prohibits cash expenditures of $100 or more?
sections.question_synthetic: When a political committee contracts with a campaign management firm, which contracts with a production firm, which pays actors in cash for commercials, must the committee report the actor payments as direct committee expenditures, and would such cash payments violate the $100+ cash expenditure prohibition?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This appears to be an opinion request meeting document from 1980 rather than a completed FPPC advice letter response. No CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section is present. The document contains the QUESTION section detailing a multi-level contracting scenario involving political committee expenditures and cash payment restrictions. The document references Government Code §84303 (agent/contractor reporting) and §84300(a) (cash expenditure prohibition) but does not provide FPPC's conclusion or advice.

embedding.summary: FPPC opinion request regarding the itemization and reporting of expenditures made through a chain of contractors (committee > campaign manager > production firm > actors), specifically addressing whether actor payments must be reported as direct committee expenditures and whether they constitute prohibited cash expenditures under Government Code §84300(a).
